# 'gs anchor create' creates a new worktree anchored at a branch.
# By default the anchor is a root anchor: a per-worktree pointer
# branch tracking the same remote trunk as the main checkout.

as 'Test <test@example.com>'
at '2026-03-29T14:00:00Z'

# setup
cd repo
git init
git commit --allow-empty -m 'Initial commit'
gs repo init

# Default: a root anchor named after the worktree directory.
gs anchor create $WORK/wt1
stderr 'Created worktree'
stderr 'Created anchor wt1'

# The worktree has the anchor branch checked out (not detached).
cd $WORK/wt1
git branch --show-current
stdout 'wt1'

# --name has no effect without an anchor, so it is rejected.
cd $WORK/repo
! gs anchor create --no-anchor --name nope $WORK/wt-bad
stderr '--name has no effect with --no-anchor'

# --no-anchor preserves the legacy detached-HEAD behavior.
gs anchor create --no-anchor $WORK/wt-detached
cd $WORK/wt-detached
git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD
stdout 'HEAD'

# --name overrides the anchor branch name, and -b creates a
# tracked branch stacked on the anchor.
cd $WORK/repo
gs anchor create $WORK/wt2 --name dev -b feat1
stderr 'Created anchor dev'
stderr 'Created and checked out branch feat1'

# Verify the branch in wt2 is feat1, stacked on the dev anchor.
cd $WORK/wt2
git branch --show-current
stdout 'feat1'
gs ls
stderr 'feat1'

-- repo/dummy.txt --
dummy
